summary:
Ashurbanipal's Library (c. 668–627 BC) at Nineveh was the ancient world's first deliberately organized library — a royal collection of approximately 30,000 clay tablets assembled by the last great Neo-Assyrian king, Ashurbanipal (r. 668–627 BC), who was unusually literate for an ancient monarch and took a personal scholarly interest in collecting the accumulated knowledge of Mesopotamian civilization. Scribes were sent throughout the empire to copy texts from temple and private libraries.

The library preserved — and thereby transmitted to us — some of the most important texts of the ancient Near East, most famously the Standard Version of the Epic of Gilgamesh, the world's oldest literary epic. It also contained astronomical and astrological observations, medical texts, incantation rituals, divination manuals, historical chronicles, lexical lists for scribal education, and the mythological corpus of Babylonian and Sumerian literature. When Nineveh was sacked in 612 BC, the library's clay tablets were fired in the conflagration — accidentally preserving them far better than they would have survived otherwise. Excavated by Austen Henry Layard and Hormuzd Rassam in the 1840s–1850s, the tablets are now largely in the British Museum, where their decipherment has revolutionized understanding of ancient Near Eastern civilization.
